entities.label: undergraduate students in medical or health care professions education
entities.extracted_text: Casebook is a tablet-based, interactive Virtual Patient teaching application for undergraduate students of medicine.

entities.label: real-life scenarios, including simulation and simulated patients
entities.extracted_text: The foremost aim of the Casebook Virtual Patient application is to help to teach clinical diagnostic reasoning and decision-making skills to undergraduate students of medicine through the examination of real patient cases.

entities.label: multiple relevant resources
entities.extracted_text: Casebook emphasises the use of pre-existing electronic health record data as the basis for case creation.

entities.label: build understanding
entities.extracted_text: This experience builds pattern recognition, meaning the more experience a student has, the more likely they will make a correct diagnosis from the data available to them.

entities.label: positive impact on learning
entities.extracted_text: Learning theory research has shown that students learn decision-making skills best when they have access to multitudes of patient cases and it is this plurality that allows students to develop their illness scripts effectively.

relationships.evidence_quote: The foremost aim of the Casebook Virtual Patient application is to help to teach clinical diagnostic reasoning and decision-making skills to undergraduate students of medicine through the examination of real patient cases.
Casebook emphasises the use of pre-existing electronic health record data as the basis for case creation.
This experience builds pattern recognition, meaning the more experience a student has, the more likely they will make a correct diagnosis from the data available to them.

Learning theory research has shown that students learn decision-making skills best when they have access to multitudes of patient cases and it is this plurality that allows students to develop their illness scripts effectively.

relationships.evidence_quote: Schema-based instruction may alter knowledge organisation and diagnostic reasoning strategies through the provision of structured knowledge to novice trainees.
the provision of schemas is hypothesised to aid in the development of compiled knowledge because schemas present the information in a structured, clinically relevant way.
This study provides important proof-of-concept for a schema-based approach and suggests there is substantial benefit to using this approach with novice trainees.
Students receiving schema-based instruction performed better on structured knowledge questions and diagnostic success was higher in the schema-based instruction group for taught lesions.
